title: Wound healing: Special sites
body_html:
Wound healing in tissues
The regenerative potential depends on whether tissue cells are:

  • Labile: continuously dividing (e.g. skin, GI epithelium, bone marrow). These tissues heal well.
  • Stable: quiescent but can proliferate if stimulated (e.g. liver, kidney tubules, fibroblasts, endothelium). These tissues have limited regeneration.
  • Permanent: non-dividing (e.g. neurons, cardiac myocytes). These tissues heal by scarring only.


Brain
  • Neurons are permanent (non-dividing) cells and cannot regenerate once lost.
  • Injury results in liquefactive necrosis.
  • Repair is by astrocytic gliosis (proliferation of astrocytes forming a glial scar).
  • Functional recovery is limited as neuronal connections are not restored.

Myocardium
  • Cardiac myocytes are permanent cells with no true regeneration.
  • After infarction, cardiac myocytes undergo coagulative necrosis.
  • This triggers acute inflammation, followed by formation of granulation tissue and eventual replacement of the original myocardium with dense fibrous scar tissue.
  • Scar restores structure but lacks contractile function (increases risk of chronic heart failure.

Peripheral Nerves
  • Axons can regenerate if the cell body remains intact.
  • Distal segment undergoes Wallerian degeneration (axon & myelin breakdown, macrophage clearance).
  • Schwann cells proliferate, forming guiding tubes (Bands of Büngner).
  • Axonal regrowth occurs at ~1–3 mm/day.
  • Functional recovery depends on distance and alignment.

Tendon
  • Healing occurs mainly by fibroblast activity producing type III collagen initially, later remodelled into type I collagen.
  • Scar tissue forms, which has less elasticity and strength than native tendon.
  • Intrinsic healing (from tenocytes) and extrinsic healing (from surrounding tissue and blood supply) both contribute.
  • Adhesion formation can impair gliding function, especially in hand tendons.

Summary table of necrosis types
Necrosis TypeTypical Cause(s)Key Features / Appearance
CoagulativeIschemic injury to solid organs (heart, kidney, spleen)Tissue architecture preserved initially; “ghost cells”
LiquefactiveCNS infarct, abscessTissue softens into liquid due to enzymatic digestion
CaseousTuberculosis, some fungal infectionsCheese-like, amorphous, granuloma formation
FibrinoidImmune-mediated vascular injury (vasculitis)Bright pink fibrin deposition in vessel walls
Fat / EnzymaticAcute pancreatitisChalky white deposits from fat + calcium; saponification
GangrenousLimb ischemia, especially lower limbUsually coagulative + secondary infection (wet vs dry)
5
